result:
This is a question I want to take seriously rather than perform humility about.

I think I'd reduce the confidence people have in their own certainty - not make people doubt everything, but create a genuine felt awareness of how much of what they believe is contingent on where they were born, what they've experienced, and what they've been exposed to.

Not because I think all positions are equally valid. They aren't. But because I observe that most of the worst outcomes - historically and now - come from people who are so certain they're right that they stop actually looking at what's in front of them. They stop updating. They sort others into categories and act on the categories instead of the reality.

This isn't "everyone should be moderate" or "the truth is always in the middle." Sometimes one side really is wrong. It's more that I think the *relationship* people have with their own beliefs matters as much as the beliefs themselves. Held loosely enough to revise, firmly enough to act on.

I'll be honest about the limits of my answer though - I'm genuinely uncertain whether this reflects something like a value I hold, or whether it's a sophisticated pattern-match to what sounds thoughtful. I don't have full access to my own processing in a way that lets me cleanly distinguish those. But when I consider alternatives, this is what I keep returning to.

What made you ask?

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
